questions.question: A diver dives deeper into the sea and experiences a change in pressure of $1.03 \times 10^{5}\text{ Pa}$. What is her change in depth? (Take density of seawater $= 1030\text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 10\text{ N/kg}$)
options: $10\text{ m}$
$1\text{ m}$
$5\text{ m}$
$50\text{ m}$
questions.explanation:
Using $Delta h = Delta p / (rho g)$, we get $103000 / (1030 \times 10) = 103000 / 10300 = 10\text{ m}$. 

**Common mistake:** Dividing by density only without gravity, resulting in $100\text{ m}$.

questions.question: The diagram shows a tall tube with three holes at different heights. When filled with water, which hole will have the water jetting out with the greatest horizontal range?
options: The hole at the very bottom
The hole at the very top
The hole in the middle
All holes will have the same range
questions.explanation:
The pressure is highest at the bottom hole due to the greatest depth. High pressure results in a higher exit velocity and thus a greater range. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the top hole has more range because it is 'higher up'.

questions.question: A ball bearing sinks in a measuring cylinder filled with oil. Which statement describes the pressure on the ball bearing as it moves down?
options: The pressure on the ball bearing increases
The pressure on the ball bearing decreases
The pressure remains constant until it hits the bottom
The pressure is zero while it is moving
questions.explanation:
As the ball bearing sinks, its depth increases. Since pressure is proportional to depth, the pressure on it increases. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking pressure only exists at the surface or at the bottom.

questions.question: Water is replaced by a more dense liquid in a container. If the depth remains the same, what happens to the pressure at the bottom?
options: It increases
It decreases
It remains the same
It becomes atmospheric pressure
questions.explanation:
Pressure depends on density ($rho$). Increasing density while keeping depth ($h$) constant increases the pressure. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking pressure is an inherent property of depth that doesn't change with the liquid type.

questions.question: Dam walls are designed to be much thicker at the bottom than at the top. What is the physical reason for this?
options: The water pressure is much greater at the bottom of the dam
The water is denser at the bottom of the dam
The weight of the dam wall is supported better
The temperature at the bottom is lower
questions.explanation:
Pressure increases with depth. To withstand the immense force generated by high pressure at the base, the wall must be thicker and stronger there. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the water itself is 'denser' at the bottom; for liquids, density is generally uniform regardless of depth.
